subject: ap-comparative-government
unit_name: Party and Electoral Systems and Citizen Organizations

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The complete set of rules that govern how votes are cast, counted, and converted into seats or offices.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An electoral district that elects exactly one representative, with the highest vote-getter winning.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A plurality voting rule in which the candidate with the most votes wins—no majority required; used for the UK House of Commons.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An electoral rule in which parties win seats in proportion to their share of the total vote, using multimember districts.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A voting system that combines single-member districts and proportional representation in the same legislature; used in Russia and Mexico.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A form of proportional representation in which voters choose a party and seats are allocated by party vote share from a ranked list of candidates.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A second round of voting held when no candidate wins an outright majority in the first round; used in Iranian and Russian presidential elections.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A minimum vote-share percentage a party must reach to receive seats; used in Russia's State Duma proportional representation portion to limit small parties.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A rule requiring that a specified share of candidates or legislators be women; Mexico's party list system uses this to increase female representation.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's unique presidential election rule requiring the winner to receive at least 25% of the vote in two-thirds of states, not just the most votes nationally.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A process in which ordinary voters elect delegates who then choose representatives at a higher level; used for China's National People's Congress.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's 12-member body that vets all candidates for the Majles and presidency to ensure compliance with Islamic values; half its members are appointed by the Supreme Leader.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's independent election management body created during democratic transition to reduce fraud and build confidence in elections.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's independent election management body, established to reduce fraud and promote transparent electoral competition.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A method of filling legislative seats by selection rather than election; used in the UK House of Lords and Russia's Federation Council.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A political system in which only one party is legally permitted to hold power; China is the primary course-country example.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China's sole ruling party since 1949; controls all branches of government and the military while allowing eight minor parties to exist under its supervision.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A system where multiple parties may legally exist but one party consistently wins elections and controls government; Russia and historically Mexico are examples.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A party system in which three or more parties regularly compete for and win legislative seats; Mexico and Nigeria are the primary course-country examples.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A political party that deliberately seeks voters across many demographic groups with a broad, ideologically flexible platform.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The practice in which legislators vote in line with their party's official position; especially strong in the UK House of Commons.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The informal practice by which Mexico's PRI president hand-picked the next presidential candidate, abolished as part of Mexico's democratic transition.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The dominant party that ruled Mexico for most of the 20th century; associated with one-party corporatist rule before transitioning to multiparty competition.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The center-right Mexican party whose candidate Vicente Fox won the presidency in 2000, ending PRI dominance.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: One of Nigeria's two dominant parties; controlled the presidency from 1999 to 2015.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The Nigerian party that came to power in 2015 when Muhammadu Buhari defeated the incumbent, representing a landmark democratic transfer of power.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A rule requiring seats or party positions to be allocated among ethnic groups; used in Nigeria's multi-ethnic federal system to ensure representation.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Russia's dominant political party, closely identified with Vladimir Putin; uses state resources and media control to maintain electoral dominance.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A broad, collective effort by large numbers of people to achieve significant social or political change; typically decentralized with no formal hierarchy.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An organization explicitly formed to represent and advocate for a specific policy issue or constituency, distinct from broader social movements.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An interest group model in which multiple autonomous groups compete freely for policy influence without state direction or licensing.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An interest group model in which the state controls access to policy making by relying on state-sanctioned groups or single peak associations to represent key sectors.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: In a corporatist system, the one officially recognized organization per sector (such as one official labor federation) that channels that sector's interests to the state.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A social movement that originates at the local or community level and builds upward toward broader political impact.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The gap between a party's share of votes and its share of seats; typically high under FPTP and lower under proportional representation.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The principle that a single representative is directly answerable to a defined geographic constituency; strongest in single-member district systems.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Legal rules specifying the minimum number of signatures, members, or geographic presence a party must demonstrate to appear on a ballot; used in Russia to restrict opposition.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Elections held by authoritarian regimes not primarily to transfer power but to generate a veneer of popular consent; Russia is the key course-country example.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigerian writer and activist who led the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People (MOSOP) and was executed by the Sani Abacha military government in 1995, becoming an international symbol of environmental rights.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The National Action Party (PAN) candidate whose victory in Mexico's 2000 presidential election ended over 70 years of PRI one-party dominance, marking Mexico's democratic transition.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The public spokesperson and military leader of the Zapatista (EZLN) uprising in Chiapas, Mexico, who became an iconic figure of indigenous rights and anti-globalization movements.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The leader of the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People whose execution by Nigeria's military government in 1995 drew international condemnation and intensified global attention to Niger Delta oil rights.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The 2009 protest movement in Iran that challenged the results of the presidential election, alleging widespread fraud; suppressed by state security forces.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The 1994 armed indigenous rebellion in Chiapas, Mexico, that launched on the same day NAFTA took effect, protesting socioeconomic inequality and the impact of trade liberalization on rural communities.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's militant group that attacked oil infrastructure in the Niger Delta to pressure the government for equitable revenue redistribution to Delta communities.
accepted_answers: Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's non-violent social movement that protested Shell Oil's extraction practices in Ogoni lands; its leader was executed by the military government in 1995.
accepted_answers: Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An Islamist insurgency in northern Nigeria that seeks to establish an Islamic state; classified by the CED as a social movement turned armed group.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The 2000 Mexican presidential election in which PAN candidate Vicente Fox defeated the PRI for the first time in over 70 years, marking the end of one-party dominance.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The 2015 Nigerian election in which Muhammadu Buhari and the APC defeated the incumbent PDP president—the first peaceful transfer of power between parties in Nigeria's history.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Domestic protests inside Russia against the State Duma's passage of legislation restricting same-sex couples, representing civil society resistance to state-imposed social restrictions.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's institutional reform that abolished the president's informal power to hand-pick the next presidential candidate, weakening PRI's grip on succession.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's gradual shift from PRI one-party dominance toward genuine multiparty competition through privatization, decentralization, and creation of an independent election commission.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A political analyst argues that the UK's electoral system tends to produce two large dominant parties while Mexico's system promotes multiparty competition. Which of the following best explains this difference?

choices.text: The UK uses single-member district plurality elections, which reward geographic vote concentration and penalize smaller parties, while Mexico uses a mixed system with proportional representation seats.
choices.explanation: FPTP in single-member districts systematically over-rewards major parties and squeezes out smaller parties; Mexico's PR component allows smaller parties to win seats proportional to their vote share, producing multiparty competition.

choices.text: The UK bans minor parties from registering, whereas Mexico's constitution requires at least three parties to compete in every election.
choices.explanation: The UK does not ban minor parties; several minor parties contest elections and some win seats. Mexico has no constitutional minimum-party rule.

choices.text: Mexico's presidential election uses a runoff system that encourages more parties to enter the first round, while the UK's parliamentary system naturally produces two parties.
choices.explanation: Mexico's president is elected by plurality, not runoff. The driver of Mexico's multiparty system is the PR component of its legislative elections, not the presidential rules.

choices.text: Nigeria introduced ethnic quotas into Mexico's party system as part of a bilateral democratic reform process.
choices.explanation: This is fictional. Ethnic quotas are a Nigerian party system feature; Mexico's multiparty shift resulted from domestic reforms including INE creation and el dedazo elimination.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which country's presidential election rule is unique among the six course countries in requiring the winner to receive a specific share of votes in a minimum number of sub-national units, not just the most votes nationally?

choices.text: Nigeria
choices.explanation: Nigeria requires the presidential winner to receive at least 25% of the vote in two-thirds of states—a rule reflecting Nigeria's federal character and need for geographically broad mandates.

choices.text: Iran
choices.explanation: Iran requires an absolute majority nationally (not a geographic distribution requirement), with a runoff if no candidate clears 50%.

choices.text: Mexico
choices.explanation: Mexico's president is elected by a plurality of the national popular vote—the candidate with the most votes wins regardless of regional distribution.

choices.text: Russia
choices.explanation: Russia requires an absolute majority nationally, with a runoff between the top two candidates if no one clears 50%. There is no sub-national distribution requirement.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's Guardian Council plays a role in the electoral process that most directly limits which democratic principle?

choices.text: Competitive elections, because the Guardian Council vets and disqualifies candidates who do not support Islamic values before any voting occurs.
choices.explanation: By excluding reformists and candidates deemed un-Islamic from the ballot, the Guardian Council restricts who can compete, making Iran's elections non-competitive in the democratic sense—voters choose among pre-approved candidates only.

choices.text: Voter turnout, because the Guardian Council requires all citizens to vote or face financial penalties.
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council vets candidates, not voters. Iran does not impose legal penalties for not voting.

choices.text: Legislative power, because the Guardian Council has the authority to dissolve the Majles at any time.
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council's electoral role is candidate vetting, not dissolving the legislature. The Supreme Leader holds broader dissolution-related powers.

choices.text: Federalism, because the Guardian Council appoints regional governors to oversee the election process.
choices.explanation: Iran is a unitary state; the Guardian Council's role is candidate vetting for national elections, not regional governance.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why Russia is classified as a dominant party system rather than a genuine multiparty democracy, even though multiple parties compete in its elections?

choices.text: Russia uses registration requirements, threshold rules, media exclusion, and selective court decisions to systematically disadvantage opposition parties and ensure United Russia's dominance.
choices.explanation: The CED (PAU-4.A.3) lists six specific mechanisms Russia uses to engineer dominance while maintaining formal multiparty appearances, making it a case of 'managed democracy' or competitive authoritarianism.

choices.text: Russia's constitution explicitly prohibits opposition parties from holding any government office at any level.
choices.explanation: Russia does not explicitly ban opposition parties by law; instead it uses informal and procedural tools to marginalize them while maintaining the appearance of legal competition.

choices.text: Russian citizens prefer United Russia because of its communist ideology and support for central planning.
choices.explanation: United Russia is a nationalist party, not ideologically communist. The CED explains Russia's dominant-party status through institutional manipulation, not genuine popular preference analysis.

choices.text: Russia adopted the Chinese one-party system model after 2000 and formally banned all other parties.
choices.explanation: Russia did not adopt China's system. China formally permits only one ruling party; Russia allows multiple registered parties but engineers dominance through procedural tools.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A political scientist writes: 'In this system, the state does not rely on autonomous competing groups—instead it grants official recognition to a limited set of organizations and channels their input directly into policy.' Which interest group model does the author describe?

choices.text: Corporatism
choices.explanation: Corporatism is the model in which the state licenses and controls recognized associations (often single peak associations) to channel labor, business, and agricultural interests into policy, rather than allowing free competition among autonomous groups.

choices.text: Pluralism
choices.explanation: Pluralism involves autonomous groups competing freely for policy influence without state licensing or channeling—the opposite of what the passage describes.

choices.text: Federalism
choices.explanation: Federalism is a principle of dividing power between central and sub-national governments, not a model of interest group access.

choices.text: Presidentialism
choices.explanation: Presidentialism is a system with a separately elected executive; it is not an interest group representation model.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the Green Movement in Iran is classified as a social movement rather than an interest group?

choices.text: The Green Movement represented broad-based popular mobilization for political change—fair elections and government accountability—rather than advocacy for a specific, defined policy interest by a formally organized association.
choices.explanation: Social movements involve large, often decentralized groups pushing for broad social or political change (IEF-2.A.1); interest groups are explicitly organized to represent a specific, narrow policy interest (IEF-2.A.2). The Green Movement fits the former.

choices.text: The Green Movement was legally registered with the Iranian government as a formal advocacy association.
choices.explanation: The Green Movement was not a state-recognized formal organization; it emerged as a mass protest movement and was suppressed rather than licensed by the state.

choices.text: Interest groups in Iran are constitutionally prohibited, so any organized group automatically qualifies as a social movement.
choices.explanation: The distinction between social movements and interest groups is analytical, not a legal category defined by Iran's constitution. The CED defines them by their scope, organization, and goals.

choices.text: The Green Movement operated at the international level and therefore cannot be classified as a domestic interest group.
choices.explanation: Social movements can have international dimensions, but the Green Movement was a domestic Iranian protest. Geographic scope is not the criterion for distinguishing social movements from interest groups.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: MEND and MOSOP are both Nigerian movements concerned with oil extraction in the Niger Delta. Which of the following best describes the key difference between them according to the CED?

choices.text: MOSOP was a non-violent movement advocating for Ogoni rights, while MEND was a militant group that used attacks on oil infrastructure as a pressure tactic.
choices.explanation: The CED (IEF-2.A.3.c) explicitly distinguishes MOSOP (non-violent, led by Ken Saro-Wiwa) from MEND (militant, used pipeline sabotage and attacks). Both sought oil revenue redistribution but used different tactics.

choices.text: MEND sought political independence for the Niger Delta, while MOSOP sought redistribution of oil revenues to the federal government.
choices.explanation: Both movements sought rights and revenue redistribution for Niger Delta communities, not federal independence. MOSOP specifically advocated for the Ogoni people's environmental and economic rights.

choices.text: MOSOP operated nationally across all of Nigeria, while MEND was limited to a single state.
choices.explanation: Both MOSOP and MEND were rooted in the Niger Delta region. MOSOP focused specifically on Ogoni lands; MEND targeted oil infrastructure broadly across the Delta.

choices.text: MEND was founded by Ken Saro-Wiwa before 1990, while MOSOP emerged after his execution in 1995.
choices.explanation: Ken Saro-Wiwa founded MOSOP, not MEND. He was executed in 1995. MEND emerged in the mid-2000s as a separate militant group.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's shift from a corporatist interest group system to a pluralist system is most directly illustrated by which of the following changes?

choices.text: Privatization of state-owned enterprises and elimination of el dedazo weakened the PRI's patronage networks, reducing the state's control over officially recognized labor and business organizations.
choices.explanation: The CED (PAU-4.A.4, IEF-2.B.4) links Mexico's corporatist-to-pluralist transition directly to the dismantling of PRI-controlled state-sanctioned groups through privatization, decentralization, and party reform.

choices.text: Mexico adopted China's model of eight legally recognized minor parties to compete against the PRI in all elections.
choices.explanation: China's eight minor parties operate under CPC supervision and cannot challenge CPC rule. Mexico's multiparty shift involved genuine competition, not a Chinese-style advisory party system.

choices.text: Mexico's Guardian Council began certifying civil society organizations as official representatives of labor and agriculture.
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council is an Iranian institution that vets electoral candidates—it has no role in Mexico. This confuses two entirely different countries and institutions.

choices.text: Mexico imposed ethnic quotas requiring all interest groups to include representatives from indigenous communities.
choices.explanation: Ethnic quotas are a Nigerian party system feature. Mexico's transition involved breaking corporatist controls, not creating ethnic quota mandates for interest groups.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A student claims: 'Proportional representation always produces better democratic outcomes than single-member district systems.' Which of the following provides the strongest counterargument to this claim?

choices.text: SMD systems provide stronger constituency accountability because each representative is directly linked to and answerable to a specific geographic area, strengthening the voter-representative bond.
choices.explanation: DEM-2.B.2 specifies that SMD systems provide geographic representation and direct constituency accountability. Neither system is inherently superior—they reflect different democratic values: PR maximizes proportionality while SMD maximizes local accountability.

choices.text: Proportional representation always leads to political instability because it produces too many parties to form a stable government.
choices.explanation: This is an overgeneralization not supported by the CED. Many stable democracies use PR systems. The CED does not claim PR causes instability.

choices.text: SMD systems produce higher voter turnout in every country because voters feel more personally connected to their representatives.
choices.explanation: The CED does not make this claim about turnout. The relationship between electoral systems and turnout is complex and not specified in the CED as a definitive pattern.

choices.text: No country using PR has ever successfully transitioned to democracy, so PR is associated with authoritarianism.
choices.explanation: This is factually incorrect. Many established democracies use PR systems. The CED does not support this claim.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China allows eight minor parties to legally exist alongside the Communist Party of China. Which of the following best explains the purpose these minor parties serve in China's political system?

choices.text: The minor parties participate in consultative bodies to broaden discussion, but cannot challenge CPC governance or hold independent power.
choices.explanation: PAU-4.A.2 explicitly states China allows eight other parties 'to broaden discussion and consultation' while rules ensure only the CPC controls governing power. These are consultative, not competitive, parties.

choices.text: The minor parties represent different ethnic minority groups and are allowed to win seats in the National People's Congress through direct elections.
choices.explanation: China's eight minor parties are not ethnic minority parties, and the NPC selection process is indirect. Ethnic minority representation in China works through a separate reserved-seats mechanism, not through independent parties.

choices.text: The minor parties compete in open elections against the CPC and sometimes win control of provincial governments.
choices.explanation: China's eight minor parties cannot and do not challenge CPC control. They operate under CPC supervision and have no path to independent governance.

choices.text: The existence of minor parties is a recent reform introduced after 2012 to comply with international democratic standards.
choices.explanation: China's eight minor parties have existed since the founding of the People's Republic in 1949. They are not a recent democratization reform.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Both Mexico's National Electoral Institute (INE) and Nigeria's Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) were created for similar purposes. Which of the following best describes that shared purpose?

choices.text: To reduce electoral fraud, build confidence in elections, and enhance competition as part of each country's democratic transition away from one-party or corrupt systems.
choices.explanation: DEM-2.B.4.b groups Mexico and Nigeria together as countries that 'created independent election commissions that attempt to reduce voter fraud and manipulation and enhance electoral competition' as part of their democratic transitions.

choices.text: To replace the role of the Guardian Council in vetting candidates and ensuring that all candidates meet constitutional standards.
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council is an Iranian institution that restricts ballot access. INE and INEC are designed to enhance electoral competition and reduce fraud—their purpose is the opposite of restricting candidate access.

choices.text: To manage the appointment of senators to the upper chamber in each country's bicameral legislature.
choices.explanation: INE and INEC administer competitive elections; they do not manage appointment processes. Appointment to upper chambers is handled separately (in the UK and Russia, not Mexico or Nigeria).

choices.text: To enforce ethnic quota requirements and ensure that all registered parties include candidates from minority communities.
choices.explanation: While Nigeria uses ethnic quotas in party systems, that is not the primary purpose of INEC. INE and INEC are general electoral management bodies focused on transparent elections.
